---
title: "Sending WhatsApp messages with Frida from a Mac"
description: "A reverse-engineering walkthrough: instrumenting the WhatsApp macOS app so you can send messages programmatically — no UI required — and the terminology you need along the way."
author: "Daniel Bartov"
date: "2026-05-30"
categories: [reverse-engineering, frida, ctf, macos]
---

This was a mobile reverse-engineering challenge: take the WhatsApp macOS app and
drive it from the outside so you can **send messages programmatically**, without
ever clicking anything in the UI. This post walks through how that works and
explains the jargon as it comes up — so even if you've never reversed an app
before, you can follow along.

## The tools and terms

A few things to know up front:

- **Frida** is a *dynamic instrumentation* toolkit. In plain terms, it lets you
  inject your own JavaScript into a running program and call the program's own
  functions, read its memory, and intercept its behavior while it runs. It's the
  Swiss-army knife of app reversing.
- **Mac Catalyst** is the technology that lets an iPad app run on macOS. The
  WhatsApp desktop app here is a Catalyst build, which means under the hood it's
  really the iOS app — same internal class names, same frameworks.
- **Objective-C runtime.** WhatsApp is written largely in Objective-C, a
  language where method calls are resolved at runtime by name. That's a gift for
  reversers: Frida can look up any class and call its methods by their string
  names, almost like the app handed us an API.
- **JID** (*Jabber ID*) is WhatsApp's internal address for a chat. WhatsApp's
  messaging is built on XMPP (the same protocol behind Jabber chat), and every
  conversation — a person or a group — is identified by a JID that looks like an
  email address, e.g. `65614920028283@lid`. When we send a message, the JID is
  *who* we're sending it to.

## The plan

We don't want to reimplement WhatsApp's encryption or network protocol — that's
a losing battle. Instead we let the real app do all the heavy lifting and just
ask it, from the inside, to send a message for us. With Frida injected, we can
reach into the app, grab the objects it already uses to send messages, and call
the send method ourselves.

## The catch: thread context

The first naive attempt crashes the app instantly with a **`SIGABRT`** (an abort
signal — the program intentionally killing itself because an internal sanity
check failed).

The reason is that WhatsApp's send code asserts it's running inside one of the
app's own *work queues* — a specific background thread the app uses to serialize
its messaging work. (A **dispatch queue** is just a managed line of tasks that
run one at a time on a background thread.) Call the send function from the wrong
thread and the app notices the missing context and aborts to protect itself.

The fix is to not fight it. WhatsApp exposes a callback,
`runWhenConnected:`, that hands a block of code to the connection's own work
queue and runs it there. By wrapping our send in that callback, our code runs in
exactly the context the app expects — the assertion passes, nothing crashes, and
we can fire a message on demand.

## Driving it

Everything is wrapped in a small interactive Python shell that launches or
attaches to WhatsApp, injects the Frida agent, and drops you into a prompt:

```sh
# interactive shell
python3 wa_shell.py

# or one-shot from the command line — send one message and exit
python3 wa_shell.py --send "65614920028283@lid" "hello from frida"
```

Inside the shell, the helpers read almost like a chat API:

- `send_text(jid, text)` — send a text message to the chat identified by `jid`
- `send_image(jid, path, caption)` — send an image
- `set_jid(jid)` — filter live output to a single chat
- `status()` — sanity-check that the internal send objects resolved correctly

So sending a message is just:

```python
send_text("65614920028283@lid", "sent straight from Frida")
```

A couple of quirks worth knowing:

- The **`jid`** is that internal chat address from earlier — not a phone number.
  The `@lid` suffix is one of WhatsApp's addressing schemes for an identity.
- Text sending only works for a chat that **already has an existing
  conversation**, because the code looks up an existing chat *session* (the
  established cryptographic conversation state with that contact) rather than
  starting a fresh one.
- Images need to be **"armed"** once — send a single image from the UI first —
  before the programmatic image path will work.

## Takeaway

The recurring lesson in app reversing: you rarely need to beat the app at its
own game. It already knows how to encrypt, address, and send a message — your
job is just to find the right internal lever and pull it. And when the app
fights back with self-protective assertions, the cleanest move is to borrow its
own machinery (here, its work queue) rather than work around it.
